text:
Audio: Why do we build models? And for a lot of people in computing. The only thing that matters in our lives is this. We just want to make predictions. But in data science... We often do science. In which case the model isn't the end goal. The predictions are at the end goal. It's trying to understand something at the world in the model. Is a way to understand something about the world. In day 8 when it was first introduced we had these two definitions prediction in front. We we define this different. So, prediction is a task of using our model to make predictions in the name for some response some output on unseen data. And then we define inference of this separate thing. Using our model. To draw conclusions about the underlying relationships between our features and the response. Today... We're going to focus on this side of the picture. The last few lectures all the analysis, we're trying to understand this side of the picture. However, I have to catch you up with industry jargon. The tech world has not not read our text and in fact I kind of agree with them. All of this this whole thing. is called inference outside of Berkeley. Here we work through a simple example of the bootstap when estimating the relationship between miles per gallon and the weight of a vehicle.
Suppose we collected a sample of 20 cars from a population. For the purposes of this demo we will assume that the seaborn dataset is the population. The following
is a visualization of our sample:
[2]:

sample_size = 100
mpg = sns. load_dataset ('mpg' )
print("Full Data Size:", len(mpg))
mpg_sample = mpg.sample(sample_size)
print("Sample Size:", len(mpg_sample) )
px. scatter(mpg_sample, x='weight', y='mpg', trendline='ols', width=800)

This requires all my boot shop samples. That's all the data that went into make this picture. I want to compute a lower and upper percentile. So if I have a 95 comes over. I have 100 miles 95 is 5. We're gonna take... 5 over 2 That's my lower percentile. So, 5 over 2 is... 2.5 so take off 2.5 from one side I get that add back up to five so I'm going to take off 2.5 from the other side. It's like trimming off these tails right here. Shoop. Can we just take my data? using the num pi percentile function it's going to sort my data and walk in 2.5% of the way and pick up that piece of data and walk all the way to the other end so it's 97.5. that piece of data. That number. Those are my lower and upper confidence rules. What does that look like? So you're two numbers. My two numbers. Now does this contain zero? Now Does not contain zero. Video: 1: File

Computing a Bootstrap CI
We can compute the CI using the percentiles of the empirical distribution:
[10]: def bootstrap_ci(bootstrap_samples, confidence_level=95) :
Returns the confidence interval for the bootstrap samples.
lower_percentile = (100 - confidence_level) / 2
upper_percentile = 100 - lower_percentile
# using numpy percentile function to compute ci
return np. percentile(bootstrap_samples, [lower_percentile, upper_percentile] )

Computing a Bootstrap CI
We can compute the CI using the percentiles of the empirical distribution:
[10]: def bootstrap_ci(bootstrap_samples, confidence_level=95) :
Returns the confidence interval for the bootstrap samples.
lower_percentile = (100 - confidence_level) / 2
upper_percentile = 100 - lower_percentile
# using numpy percentile function to compute ci
return np. percentile(bootstrap_samples, [lower_percentile, upper_percentile] )

Audio: Which up isn't perfect. But it's pretty close. And when we see curves that look kind of like dull curves and they're pretty concentrated and there's no weird tails like, you know, like some of these here. Those are generally good at good things to apply but you should have to. Reconstraight. Maybe I'm sitting here like that doesn't look great. A kind of surprise, why might this happen? It speaks to an issue of boot drive. Maybe there's some extreme cars that are super light and very efficient. You have extreme data points and you think about this whole bootstrapping process. If there's that one... Massive mansion in your housing dataset. Are you doing this re-sampling? And in your original sample, unfortunately you didn't collect that massive house. But if you go back to the population and you reset sample many many times and there is that ridiculous house and that gets in one of your samples everything is messed up now. So we are kind of sensitive to outliers when we use these techniques. So you want to... Study situations where the statistic is robust to these outliers. That's why we do allow that bias control. That variance control from our earlier lecture. What our statistics are well behaved. And not so sensitive outliers, these composables can be very accurate. This isn't that bad, and even here we're still very far from zero. So I'm pretty confident. that the slope is negative. Questions? Video: 1: File

If there's that one... Massive mansion in your housing dataset. Are you doing this re-sampling? And in your original sample, unfortunately you didn't collect that massive house. But if you go back to the population and you reset sample many many times and there is that ridiculous house and that gets in one of your samples everything is messed up now. , 9: Bootstrap Discussion
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
The bootstrapped sampling distribution of an estimator approximates the
sampling distribution of that estimator.
The center and spread are approximations
The center of the bootstrapped distribution is the estimator applied to our original sample.
Bootstrap doesn't improve your estimate!
The quality of our bootstrapped distribution depends on the quality of our original sample.
Works better for large samples
Works best when population distribution is not heavily skewed (no outliers)
Works best when estimator is "low variance" (insensitive to extreme values)

The quality of our bootstrapped distribution depends on the quality of our original sample.
Works better for large samples
Works best when population distribution is not heavily skewed (no outliers)
Works best when estimator is "low variance" (insensitive to extreme values)

1. Build a model that adjusts PurpleAir (PA) measurements based

2. Karoline Barkjohn, Brett Gannt, and Andrea Clements from the
US Environmental Protection Agency developed a model to
improve the PuprleAir measurements from the AQS sensor
measurements by incorporating Relative Humidity:
Demo
PA ~ 00 + 01AQS + O2RH
Barkjohn and group's work is now used in the official US government maps,
like the AirNow Fire and Smoke map, includes both AQS and PurpleAir
sensors, and applies Barkjohn's correction to the PurpleAir data.

After we build the model that adjusts the PurpleAir measurements using AQS, we then flip the model around and use it to predict the true air quality in the future from
PurpleAir measurements when we don't have a nearby AQS instrument. This is a calibration scenario. Since the AQS measurements are close to the truth, we fit the
more variable PurpleAir measurements to them; this is the calibration procedure. Then, we use the calibration curve to correct future PurpleAir measurements. This
two-step process is encapsulated in the simple linear model and its flipped form below.

Inverse regression:
· First, we fit a line to predict a PA measurement from the ground truth, as recorded by an AQS instrument:
PA ~ 00 + 01AQS
. Next, we flip the line around to use a PA measurement to predict the air quality,
True Air Quality ~ - 00/01 +1/01PA
Why perform this "inverse regression"?
Intuitively, AQS measurements are "true" and have no error.
. A linear model takes a "true" x value input and minimizes the error in the y direction.
· Algebraically identical, but statistically different.

y Gonzalez
1. Build a model that adjusts PurpleAir (PA) measurements based
on nearby AQS measurements (AQS, true air quality).
PA ~00 + 01AQS
2. Karoline Barkjohn, Brett Gannt, and Andrea Clements from the
US Environmental Protection Agency developed a model to
improve the PuprleAir measurements from the AQS sensor
measurements by incorporating Relative Humidity:
Demo
PA ~ 00 + 01AQS + O2RH
Barkjohn and group's work is now used in the official US government maps,
like the AirNow Fire and Smoke map, includes both AQS and PurpleAir
sensors, and applies Barkjohn's correction to the PurpleAir data.

Inverse regression:
· First, we fit a line to predict a PA measurement from the ground truth, as recorded by an AQS instrument:
PA ~ 00 + 01AQS
. Next, we flip the line around to use a PA measurement to predict the air quality,
True Air Quality ~ - 00/01 +1/01PA
Why perform this "inverse regression"?
Intuitively, AQS measurements are "true" and have no error.
. A linear model takes a "true" x value input and minimizes the error in the y direction.
· Algebraically identical, but statistically different.

(Ch12, Ch 17)
A linear model takes a "true" x value input and minimizes
the error in the y direction.
Algebraically identical, but statistically different.

csv_file = 'data/Full24hrdataset . csv. gz'
usecols = ['Date', 'ID', 'region', 'PM25FM' ,| 'PM25cf1', 'TempC', 'RH', 'Dewpoint']
full_df = pd. read_csv(csv_file, usecols=usecols, parse_dates=['Date'] ). dropna()
full_df . columns = ['date', 'id', 'region', 'pm25aqs', 'pm25pa', 'temp', 'rh', 'dew' ]
full_df = full_df [ ( full_df ['pm25aqs' ] < 50) ]
# drop dates with issues in the data
bad_dates = pd. to_datetime( ['2019-08-21', '2019-08-22', '2019-09-24'])
GA = full_df [ ( full_df ['id'] == 'GA1' ) & (~full_df ['date' ] . isin(bad_dates) ) ]
GA = GA. sort_values("pm25aqs")
display ( full_df ["region"] . value_counts ( ) )
display(GA. head ( ) )
print ("Number of Rows:", GA. shape [0])
Inverse Regression
After we build the model that adjusts the PurpleAir measurements using AQS, we then flip the model around and use it to predict the true air quality in the future from
PurpleAir measurements when we don't have a nearby AQS instrument. This is a calibration scenario. Since the AQS measurements are close to the truth, we fit the
more variable PurpleAir measurements to them; this is the calibration procedure. Then, we use the calibration curve to correct future PurpleAir measurements. This
two-step process is encapsulated in the simple linear model and its flipped form below.
Inverse regression:

Inverse regression:
· First, we fit a line to predict a PA measurement from the ground truth, as recorded by an AQS instrument:
PA ~ 00 + 01AQS
. Next, we flip the line around to use a PA measurement to predict the air quality,
True Air Quality ~ - 00/01 +1/01PA
Why perform this "inverse regression"?
Intuitively, AQS measurements are "true" and have no error.
. A linear model takes a "true" x value input and minimizes the error in the y direction.
· Algebraically identical, but statistically different.

. Next, we flip the line around to use a PA measurement to predict the air quality,

Why perform this "inverse regression"?
Intuitively, AQS measurements are "true" and have no error.
. A linear model takes a "true" x value input and minimizes the error in the y direction.
· Algebraically identical, but statistically different.

I also, as I'm going to, in a moment, add more features. I want to understand how those features, how important they are in this model. Just to show you if I did the non-inverse version, so if I just did a least gross fit, I get a slightly different line. This is again accounting for that difference in variability in the AQS system versus the for a player system. So the green line is what you would have thought to do, just fit the model that goes like this. And the red line is this weird thing where I flip the axes, solve an equation, and like solve, fit my model and then inverted my equation. You're not quite the same. And again in these situations in science you often use this inverse fit approach. Now it's the actual science. So that air quality and the way these laser systems work, they measure stuff in the air, having humidity likely has an effect on these laser readings. And so we have humidity data and we want to know do I need to use that humidity data to control my estimates to get a better relationship between the purple air and the US government EPA system. Let's do this. We're making good progress. Let's take a two-minute break and then we'll jump into the analysis for part two. Video: 1: File

The Barkjohn et al. model with Relative Humidity
ETVE F
Karoline Barkjohn, Brett Gannt, and Andrea Clements from the US Environmental Protection Agency developed a model to improve the PuprleAir measurements from
the AQS sensor measurements. Arkjohn and group's work was so successful that, as of this writing, the official US government maps, like the AirNow Fire and Smoke
map, includes both AQS and PurpleAir sensors, and applies Barkjohn's correction to the PurpleAir data.
PA ~ 0+ 01AQS+ 2RH
The model that Barkjohn settled on incorporated the relative humidity:
[ ]: model_h = lm. LinearRegression( ) . fit(GA[['pm25aqs', 'rh' ] ], GA['pm25pa' ] )
[theta_1, theta_2], theta_0 = model_h. coef _, model_h. intercept_
print (f"True Air Quality Estimate = {-theta_0/theta_1:1.2} + {1/theta_1 :. 2}PA + {-theta_2/theta_1 :. 2}RH")
[ ]:
fig = px. scatter(GA, x='pm25aqs', y='pm25pa', width=800)
fig. add_scatter(x=xtest ['pm25aqs'], y=model. predict(xtest [ ['pm25aqs' ] ]), mode='lines',
name="Least Squares Fit")
fig. add_scatter(x=GA["pm25aqs"], y=model_h. predict(GA[ ['pm25aqs', 'rh' ] ] ), mode='lines+markers',
marker_size=5, name="Least Squares Fit with RH")

map, includes both AQS and PurpleAir sensors, and applies Barkjohn's correction to the PurpleAir data.
PA ~ 0+ 01AQS + 02RH

pm25pa
The Barkjohn et al. model with Relative Humidity
ETVE F
Karoline Barkjohn, Brett Gannt, and Andrea Clements from the US Environmental Protection Agency developed a model to improve the PuprleAir measurements from
the AQS sensor measurements. Arkjohn and group's work was so successful that, as of this writing, the official US government maps, like the AirNow Fire and Smoke
map, includes both AQS and PurpleAir sensors, and applies Barkjohn's correction to the PurpleAir data.
PA ~ 0+ 01AQS+ 2RH
The model that Barkjohn settled on incorporated the relative humidity:
[ ]: model_h = lm. LinearRegression( ) . fit(GA[['pm25aqs', 'rh' ] ], GA['pm25pa' ] )
[theta_1, theta_2], theta_0 = model_h. coef _, model_h. intercept_
print (f"True Air Quality Estimate = {-theta_0/theta_1:1.2} + {1/theta_1 :. 2}PA + {-theta_2/theta_1 :. 2}RH")
[ ]:
fig = px. scatter(GA, x='pm25aqs', y='pm25pa', width=800)
fig. add_scatter(x=xtest ['pm25aqs'], y=model. predict(xtest [ ['pm25aqs' ] ]), mode='lines',
name="Least Squares Fit")
fig. add_scatter(x=GA["pm25aqs"], y=model_h. predict(GA[ ['pm25aqs', 'rh' ] ] ), mode='lines+markers',
marker_size=5, name="Least Squares Fit with RH")
Simple

The Barkjohn et al. model with Relative Humidity
Karoline Barkjohn, Brett Gannt, and Andrea Clements from the US Environmental Protection Agency developed a model to improve the PuprleAir measurements from
the AQS sensor measurements. Arkjohn and group's work was so successful that, as of this writing, the official US government maps, like the AirNow Fire and Smoke
map, includes both AQS and PurpleAir sensors, and applies Barkjohn's correction to the PurpleAir data.
PA ~ 00+ 01AQS + 02RH
The model that Barkjohn settled on incorporated the relative humidity:
[ ]: model_h = \m. LinearRegression( ) . fit(GA[ ['pm25aqs', 'rh' ] ], GA [' pm25pa' ])
[theta_1, theta_2], theta_0 = model_h. coef _, model_h. intercept_
print(f"True Air Quality Estimate = {-theta_0/theta_1:1.2} + {1/theta_1 :. 2}PA + {-theta_2/theta_1 :. 2}RH")
[ ]:
fig = px. scatter(GA, x='pm25aqs', y='pm25pa', width=800)
fig. add_scatter(x=xtest ['pm25aqs'], y=model.predict(xtest [ ['pm25aqs' ] ] ), mode='lines',
name="Least Squares Fit")
fig. add_scatter(x=GA["pm25aqs"], y=model_h. predict(GA[['pm25aqs', 'rh '] ] ), mode='lines+markers ',
marker_size=5, name="Least Squares Fit with RH")

Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...

the AQS sensor measurements. Arkjohn and group's work was so successful that, as of this writing, the official US government maps, like the AirNow Fire and Smoke
map, includes both AQS and PurpleAir sensors, and applies Barkjohn's correction to the PurpleAir data.
PA ~ 0+ 01AQS + 02RH
The model that Barkjohn settled on incorporated the relative humidity:
[ ]: model_h = lm. LinearRegression( ) . fit(GA[ [ 'pm25aqs|' , 'rh' ] ], GA['pm25pa' ] )
[theta_1, theta_2], theta_0 = model_h. coef _, model_h. intercept_
print (f"True Air Quality Estimate = {-theta_0/theta_1:1.2} + {1/theta_1 :. 2}PA + {-theta_2/theta_1 :. 2}RH")
[ ]:

fig. add_scatter(x=xtest ['pm25aqs ' ], y=model.predict(xtest [ ['pm25aqs ' ] ] ), mode='lines',
name="Least Squares Fit")

zs = model_h.predict(pd. DataFrame({"pm25aqs": u. flatten(), "rh": v.flatten()}) )

The Data
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
The Snowy Plover is a tiny bird that lives on the coast in parts of California and elsewhere. It is so small that it is vulnerable to many predators and to people and dogs
that don't look where they are stepping when they go to the beach. It is considered endangered in many parts of the US.
The data are about the eggs and newly-hatched chicks of the Snowy Plover. Here's a parent bird and some eggs.
The data were collected at the Point Reyes National Seashore by a former student at Berkeley. The goal was to see how the size of an egg could be used to predict the
weight of the resulting chick. The bigger the newly-hatched chick, the more likely it is to survive.

The Data
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
The Snowy Plover is a tiny bird that lives on the coast in parts of California and elsewhere. It is so small that it is vulnerable to many predators and to people and dogs
that don't look where they are stepping when they go to the beach. It is considered endangered in many parts of the US.
The data are about the eggs and newly-hatched chicks of the Snowy Plover. Here's a parent bird and some eggs.
The data were collected at the Point Reyes National Seashore by a former student at Berkeley. The goal was to see how the size of an egg could be used to predict the
weight of the resulting chick. The bigger the newly-hatched chick, the more likely it is to survive.

Audio: Again, we've covered this in previous lectures, but this is saying that any set of my features can be linearly expressed as a function of my other features. If that's the case, they can be described that way. They are collinear and then I have a matrix that is not full-rank. I have all those problems with inversion and again now you know that I now have problems with the parameter inference as well. Okay. Talk about this. Yeah, so this is the punchline here as a cleanery when features can be predicted accurately by a linear function of the other features. The features have a query with others, which we saw in the correlation plot. These slopes coefficients are hard to interpret using the analysis we have. We've already discussed it's not invertible. Yeah, and then small chat is a very small changes in data sample can lead to big changes in the estimated slopes. Regularization can help with some of this, but typically, we're doing this inference, you may not actually use aggressive regularization. That's more for improving prediction stability. Good example. Yeah, we'll skip it. There's an example of the slides for those who are watching later. You can think through a simple setup where you have two exact functions of each other, and then you can choose any model parameterization where you put all the way on one feature, all the way on the other feature.
